sources.notes: Read statement and constraints: hidden bracket sequence length n <= 1000, query k <= 1000 repeated indices allowed, at most 550 queries; f(t) counts non-empty regular bracket substrings.
Used official editorial D1C1/C2/C3. Easy version finds '(' and ')' anchors, then uses small templates to determine two characters per query.

reference_candidates.notes: Candidate Luogu solution page; not consulted in this review.

annotation.summary: Interactive reconstruction of a hidden bracket sequence. Each query asks f on a chosen sequence of indices, where f counts contiguous regular bracket substrings.
annotation.constraints: n <= 1000, at most 550 queries, k <= 1000; hidden sequence contains at least one '(' and one ')'.
annotation.core_idea: First calibrate the oracle by finding one index holding '(' and one holding ')'. Then construct constant-size query templates containing two unknown positions and the anchors so that the returned value has four possible outcomes, uniquely revealing the two brackets. With roughly n/2 data queries plus calibration, the easy query limit is enough.
annotation.complexity: O(n) queries, O(n) local work besides interaction.
tricks: Calibrate the query oracle using known opposite bracket anchors.
Design a small query string whose count of regular substrings acts as a four-way code for two unknown brackets.

solution_variants.name: two-characters-per-query
solution_variants.summary: Find opposite anchors, then determine two unknown positions per query using a handcrafted four-outcome bracket template.

tags.evidence: The task is explicitly interactive; the solution is a strategy for choosing adaptive queries and decoding jury answers.
The core is to build query strings where f(t) maps bracket cases to distinguishable return values.
tags.description: Designing queries to an oracle/interactor so that the numeric or Boolean response directly encodes the desired hidden information.
tags.created_reason: The broad interactive tag does not distinguish problems whose main difficulty is constructing a decoding oracle rather than binary search or randomized probing.

tags.evidence: The algorithm is a constructive query-template strategy, not a standard offline computation.
The editorial uses binary search to find an anchor pair of opposite brackets efficiently.
The easy version only needs four-case decoding; bit-style encoding becomes more central in C2/C3.
